text: This paper examines the nuances of political rhetoric and "linguistic spin" in the 2013 United Nations General Assembly addresses delivered by Barack Obama and Hassan Rouhani. Utilizing Halliday’s Systemic Functional Linguistics (SFL) as a theoretical framework, the study investigates how both leaders employ specific grammatical choices to project ideology and exert soft power. The analysis focuses primarily on transitivity systems and modality, uncovering how the presidents use language to articulate national policies, manifest authority, and shape public perception. By deconstructing the interpersonal and ideational metafunctions of these speeches, the research demonstrates how complex political agendas are translated into accessible discourse, ultimately allowing the general public to better grasp the underlying implications of international diplomacy.
